name: Myanmar (Burma)
tagline: From the Buddhist city-states of the Irrawaddy and the temple-strewn plain of Bagan to a modern nation forged by empire, war, and a long contest between army and democracy.
summary: Myanmar's recorded history begins with the Pyu city-states of the Irrawaddy basin, where Buddhism gained its first lasting foothold in Southeast Asia, and rises to the kingdom of Pagan (Bagan), whose king Anawrahta made Theravada Buddhism the foundation of Burmese civilization. The Toungoo and Konbaung dynasties built the largest Burmese empires before the Konbaung kings lost three Anglo-Burmese Wars, ending in the British annexation of the whole country in 1885 and its rule first as a province of British India and, from 1937, as a separate colony. After Japanese occupation in the Second World War and the rise of the nationalist Aung San, Burma won independence on 4 January 1948. General Ne Win's 1962 coup opened decades of military rule punctuated by the 1988 uprising and the long pro-democracy struggle of Aung San Suu Kyi; a fragile opening after 2011 was reversed by the military's coup of 1 February 2021, and the country remains gripped by conflict in 2026.

founding.label: Independence of the Union of Burma
founding.detail: On 4 January 1948 Burma became an independent sovereign state, leaving the British Empire entirely rather than remaining within the Commonwealth. The architect of independence, the nationalist leader Aung San, had negotiated the transfer of power with British Prime Minister Clement Attlee in January 1947 but was assassinated with most of his cabinet in July 1947; the new nation was led to independence by U Nu. The date crowned a long arc of statehood reaching back through the Konbaung, Toungoo and Pagan kingdoms to the Pyu city-states, and through more than six decades of British colonial rule that had ended the Burmese monarchy in 1885. Independence proved fragile: ethnic insurgency and political instability gave way in 1962 to military rule that would shape the country for generations.

quickFacts.label: Earliest Buddhist urban culture
quickFacts.value: Pyu city-states of the Irrawaddy basin, flourishing roughly 200 BCE-900 CE

quickFacts.label: First unifying kingdom
quickFacts.value: Pagan (Bagan), founded as a power by Anawrahta (reigned 1044-77)

quickFacts.label: Last monarchy
quickFacts.value: The Konbaung dynasty, ended when the British exiled King Thibaw in 1885

quickFacts.label: Military rule begins
quickFacts.value: General Ne Win's coup of 2 March 1962

quickFacts.label: Most recent coup
quickFacts.value: The military seized power again on 1 February 2021

eras.title: The Pyu City-States and the Coming of Buddhism
eras.standfirst: In the dry plains of the Irrawaddy, the Tibeto-Burman Pyu built walled, moated cities that gave Buddhism its earliest lasting home in Southeast Asia.
body: The first urban civilization of the region was that of the Pyu, speakers of a Tibeto-Burman language who entered the Irrawaddy (Ayeyarwady) valley from the north and, over roughly a thousand years between about 200 BCE and 900 CE, raised a constellation of city-states in the dry central zone. The greatest of these were Beikthano, Halin and Sri Ksetra, each a brick-walled and moated city set amid irrigated farmland. Their excavated palace citadels, burial grounds, manufacturing sites and great brick stupas survive today as the Pyu Ancient Cities, which in 2014 became Myanmar's first UNESCO World Heritage site.

Through contact with the Indic world the Pyu embraced Buddhism, and their cities provide the earliest testimony to the religion's introduction into Southeast Asia, almost two thousand years ago. Theravada Buddhism, along with Indian scripts, ritual and statecraft, took root among the Pyu and shaped the economic, social and cultural transformations that produced the region's first large and long-lived urban settlements. The monumental stupas and water-management works of these cities mark the beginning of a religious and agricultural civilization that would define Burmese life for centuries.

Pyu power waned in the 9th century, weakened by raids from the Nanzhao kingdom to the northeast and by the southward migration of the Burman people into the valley. As the city-states declined, the Burmans gathered at a settlement on a bend of the Irrawaddy that would become Bagan. The Pyu legacy, Buddhism, irrigated rice agriculture and the city as a sacred and political center, passed to these newcomers, who would build upon it the first kingdom to unify the land.

events.title: Rise of the Pyu city-states
events.summary: Tibeto-Burman Pyu peoples establish walled, moated cities such as Beikthano, Halin and Sri Ksetra in the Irrawaddy basin, founding the region's first urban civilization.

events.title: Buddhism takes root
events.summary: The Pyu cities provide the earliest evidence of Buddhism's introduction into Southeast Asia, embedding the religion in the region's culture and statecraft.

events.title: Decline of the Pyu
events.summary: Raids from the Nanzhao kingdom and the southward migration of the Burman people erode Pyu power, clearing the way for the rise of Bagan.

pullquote.text: The Pyu cities give the earliest testimony to the introduction of Buddhism into Southeast Asia, almost two thousand years ago.
pullquote.attribution: UNESCO World Heritage Centre, on the Pyu Ancient Cities

eras.title: The Kingdom of Pagan (Bagan)
eras.standfirst: On a bend of the Irrawaddy, King Anawrahta forged the first unified Burmese kingdom and made Theravada Buddhism its soul, leaving behind the temple-covered plain of Bagan.
body: Tradition dates the founding of the city of Bagan to the 9th century, but it became a great power only under Anawrahta, who reigned from 1044 to 1077 and is counted the first king of all Myanmar. Anawrahta united the Burman heartland in the north with the Mon kingdoms of the south, and was converted to Theravada Buddhism by the Mon monk Shin Arahan. Primarily through his efforts Theravada Buddhism became the dominant religion of the country and the inspiration of its culture, displacing earlier Mahayana and Tantric forms.

Anawrahta's conquest of the Mon city of Thaton, by tradition in 1057, is said to have brought back the Buddhist scriptures, monks and craftsmen who enriched Pagan's civilization. Over the following two centuries his successors covered the plain of Bagan with an extraordinary density of stupas, temples and monasteries, financed by a wealthy agrarian economy and the merit-making piety of kings and commoners alike. The surviving monuments, more than three thousand recorded structures with their murals, sculptures and inscriptions, bear spectacular witness to the peak of Bagan civilization between the 11th and 13th centuries and were inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage site in 2019.

The kingdom's strength ebbed in the 13th century as religious endowments drained royal revenues and frontier pressures mounted. The decisive blow came from the Mongol empire of Kublai Khan: after Burmese provocations, Mongol armies invaded, and by 1287 the kingdom had effectively collapsed, with a further incursion in 1297 ending Pagan as a unifying power. The land fragmented into rival principalities, but the religious and cultural framework Anawrahta had built, Theravada Buddhism and the temple-centered kingship, endured as the foundation of every later Burmese state.

events.title: Founding of Bagan
events.summary: The city of Bagan is traditionally established on a bend of the Irrawaddy, later to become the seat of the first Burmese kingdom.

events.title: Reign of Anawrahta
events.summary: Anawrahta unifies the Burman north and the Mon south into the first Burmese kingdom and adopts Theravada Buddhism as the state religion.

events.title: Conquest of Thaton
events.summary: Anawrahta captures the Mon city of Thaton, by tradition bringing Buddhist scriptures, monks and artisans that enrich Pagan's culture.

events.title: Mongol invasion and collapse
events.summary: Armies of Kublai Khan's Mongol empire invade and shatter the kingdom; a further incursion in 1297 ends Bagan as a unifying power.

figures.name: Anawrahta
figures.blurb: The first king of a unified Myanmar, he joined the Burman north and Mon south and, converted by the monk Shin Arahan, made Theravada Buddhism the dominant religion and lasting cultural foundation of the country.

pullquote.text: Primarily through his efforts, Theravada Buddhism became the dominant religion of Myanmar and the inspiration for its culture and civilization.

eras.title: Empires Reborn: Toungoo and Konbaung
eras.standfirst: Twice after Bagan's fall the Burmese kings rebuilt great empires, under Bayinnaung of Toungoo and Alaungpaya of Konbaung, the second the largest in the country's history.
body: After more than two centuries of fragmentation, the Toungoo dynasty reunified the land in the 16th century. Its greatest ruler, Bayinnaung, who reigned from 1551 to 1581, restored the territory of old Pagan and pushed far beyond it, conquering the Shan states and the kingdom of Siam to make Toungoo Myanmar the most powerful state in mainland Southeast Asia. A devoted patron of Buddhism, he built pagodas, endowed monasteries and maintained diplomatic ties with Buddhist Ceylon. His overstretched empire fragmented after his death, and the dynasty finally fell in 1752 when its capital, Ava, was captured by Mon forces from the south.

From this collapse rose the last and, at its height, largest of the Burmese empires. In 1752 a village chief named Alaungpaya raised a rebellion against the Mon, founded the Konbaung dynasty, and within a few years had reconquered Lower Burma, capturing Pegu in 1757. His militaristic successors expanded relentlessly: they invaded Siam, annexed the Shan states, and pressed against the western frontiers of Arakan and Manipur. By the early 19th century the Konbaung empire stretched across most of mainland Southeast Asia and abutted the territory of the expanding British East India Company in Bengal.

The Konbaung kings governed a centralized Buddhist monarchy in which the king was the chief patron of the sangha, the monastic order, and the guarantor of the religion's purity. Court chronicles, law codes and a flourishing monastic education sustained a distinctive civilization. But the dynasty's aggressive westward expansion into Assam and Manipur brought it into direct collision with British India. The clash of two expanding empires along the Bay of Bengal set the stage for a series of wars that the Burmese monarchy, for all its power, would not survive.

events.title: Reign of Bayinnaung
events.summary: Bayinnaung of the Toungoo dynasty reunifies Myanmar and conquers the Shan states and Siam, building the most powerful empire in mainland Southeast Asia.

events.title: Alaungpaya founds the Konbaung dynasty
events.summary: The village chief Alaungpaya leads a revolt against Mon overlords and establishes the Konbaung dynasty, the last royal house of Burma.

events.title: Reconquest of Lower Burma
events.summary: Alaungpaya's forces capture Pegu and subdue the Mon south, reuniting the country under Konbaung rule.

events.title: Annexation of Arakan
events.summary: Konbaung expansion absorbs the kingdom of Arakan on the western coast, bringing the Burmese frontier up against British-held Bengal.

figures.name: Bayinnaung
figures.blurb: The conqueror who reunified Myanmar and subdued the Shan states and Siam, making the Toungoo realm the most powerful kingdom in mainland Southeast Asia and a great patron of Buddhism.

figures.name: Alaungpaya
figures.blurb: A village chief who rose against Mon rule to found the Konbaung dynasty in 1752 and reunify the country, founding the last and largest of the Burmese empires.

pullquote.text: Bayinnaung unified his country and conquered the Shan States and Siam, making Myanmar the most powerful kingdom in mainland Southeast Asia.

eras.title: The Anglo-Burmese Wars and British Rule
eras.standfirst: Three wars over six decades stripped the Konbaung kings of their kingdom; Britain abolished the monarchy in 1885 and ruled Burma as part of India and then as a separate colony until independence.
body: British conquest came in three stages. The First Anglo-Burmese War of 1824-26, sparked by friction along the Arakan and Assam frontiers, ended with the Treaty of Yandabo, by which Burma ceded Arakan and Tenasserim. The Second Anglo-Burmese War of 1852, provoked by British commercial and strategic interests, brought the annexation of Lower Burma, including the rice-rich Irrawaddy delta and the renamed province of Pegu. The Third Anglo-Burmese War of November 1885 lasted barely two weeks: British forces took the royal capital of Mandalay, deposed and exiled King Thibaw, and on 1 January 1886 annexed Upper Burma, extinguishing the Burmese monarchy.

Britain governed Burma as a province of its Indian Empire, detaching the state from religious affairs and so depriving the sangha of its traditional royal patronage and status. Colonial rule reoriented the economy around the export of rice, teak and, later, oil; the Irrawaddy delta was transformed into one of the world's great rice bowls, and large-scale Indian immigration reshaped commerce and labor. Yet the dismantling of the monarchy and the marginalization of Burmese elites bred deep resentment, and from the early 20th century a nationalist movement grew, rooted in Buddhist revival and in the student politics of Rangoon University.

In 1937 Burma was separated from British India and made a distinct crown colony with a measure of self-government. The Second World War then transformed the independence struggle. Japan invaded in 1942, and young nationalists led by Aung San at first allied with the Japanese before turning against them and rejoining the Allies as liberation neared. After the war Aung San emerged as the dominant national leader; in January 1947 he negotiated independence with British Prime Minister Clement Attlee, but in July 1947 he and most of his cabinet were assassinated. U Nu carried the settlement to completion, and on 4 January 1948 the Union of Burma became fully independent outside the Commonwealth.

events.title: First Anglo-Burmese War
events.summary: War over the Arakan and Assam frontiers ends with the Treaty of Yandabo (1826), forcing Burma to cede Arakan and Tenasserim to Britain.

events.title: Second Anglo-Burmese War
events.summary: British forces annex Lower Burma, including the Irrawaddy delta and the province renamed Pegu, tightening control of the coastline.

events.title: Third Anglo-Burmese War and the end of the monarchy
events.summary: Britain takes Mandalay, exiles King Thibaw, and annexes Upper Burma on 1 January 1886, abolishing the Burmese monarchy and completing the conquest.

events.title: Burma separated from British India
events.summary: Burma is made a distinct crown colony with limited self-government, administered separately from the Indian Empire.

events.title: Assassination of Aung San
events.summary: Aung San, who had negotiated independence with Britain, is assassinated with most of his cabinet months before the transfer of power.

figures.name: Aung San
figures.role: Nationalist leader and architect of independence
figures.blurb: The founding father of independent Burma, he led the wartime nationalist movement, switching from Japanese to Allied alliance, and negotiated independence with Britain in 1947 before his assassination that July.

pullquote.text: The British decision to eliminate the monarchy, sending Thibaw into exile, deprived the sangha of its traditional status and official patronage.
pullquote.attribution: Encyclopaedia Britannica, on the Third Anglo-Burmese War

eras.title: Independence, Ne Win, and Decades of Military Rule
eras.standfirst: A democratic republic gave way in 1962 to General Ne Win's army, whose isolating socialism and the suppression of the 1988 uprising made Aung San Suu Kyi the face of a long struggle for democracy.
body: The Union of Burma began independent life in 1948 under Prime Minister U Nu as a parliamentary democracy, but it was beset from the start by communist insurgency and by ethnic rebellions among the Karen, Shan and others that have never fully ceased. After a caretaker military administration in the late 1950s, the experiment in democracy ended on 2 March 1962, when General Ne Win seized power in a coup, imprisoned U Nu, and established a Revolutionary Council drawn almost entirely from the armed forces. Military rule, in one form or another, would dominate the country for the next half-century.

Ne Win imposed the Burmese Way to Socialism, nationalizing industry and commerce, expelling much of the Indian and Chinese merchant class, and sealing the country off from the world. The result was deepening poverty and isolation. By 1988 economic collapse and political repression ignited a nationwide pro-democracy uprising, the 8888 movement, which the military crushed with great bloodshed in September 1988 while the State Law and Order Restoration Council seized power. Out of this moment rose Aung San Suu Kyi, daughter of the independence hero Aung San, who became the leading voice of the democracy movement.

In 1990 the military permitted elections that Aung San Suu Kyi's National League for Democracy won overwhelmingly, but the generals annulled the result and kept her under house arrest for much of the next two decades; in 1991 she was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize. The junta, which renamed the country Myanmar in 1989, ruled through repression and isolation, weathered international sanctions, and brutally suppressed a monk-led 'Saffron Revolution' in 2007. Only in 2008, after a devastating cyclone, did it advance a new constitution and a 'roadmap' that would, in 2011, hand nominal power to a quasi-civilian government.

events.title: Independence of the Union of Burma
events.summary: Burma becomes a fully independent republic outside the Commonwealth under Prime Minister U Nu, soon facing communist and ethnic insurgencies.

events.title: Ne Win's coup
events.summary: General Ne Win overthrows the elected government, imprisons U Nu, and inaugurates decades of military rule under the Burmese Way to Socialism.

events.title: The 8888 uprising
events.summary: A nationwide pro-democracy uprising is crushed by the military, which forms a new junta; Aung San Suu Kyi emerges as the democracy movement's leader.

events.title: Annulled NLD election victory
events.summary: Aung San Suu Kyi's National League for Democracy wins elections in a landslide, but the junta ignores the result and detains her for years.

events.title: The Saffron Revolution
events.summary: Buddhist monks lead mass protests against the regime that are violently suppressed, drawing renewed international condemnation.

figures.name: Ne Win
figures.blurb: The general who seized power in 1962 and ruled until 1988, combining a repressive military dictatorship with the isolating socialist program known as the Burmese Way to Socialism.

figures.name: Aung San Suu Kyi
figures.blurb: Daughter of the independence hero Aung San, she led a decades-long nonviolent struggle for democracy, won the 1991 Nobel Peace Prize, and spent years under house arrest before later entering government.

pullquote.text: On 2 March 1962, Ne Win carried out a coup d'etat, imprisoning U Nu and combining a repressive military dictatorship with a socialist economic program.
pullquote.attribution: Encyclopaedia Britannica, on U Ne Win

eras.title: Opening, the Rohingya Crisis, and the 2021 Coup
eras.standfirst: A decade of cautious opening brought Aung San Suu Kyi to power and the Rohingya catastrophe to the world's attention, before the army seized control again in 2021 and plunged Myanmar into renewed war.
body: In 2011 a quasi-civilian government under former general Thein Sein launched unexpected reforms: it freed political prisoners, eased censorship, signed cease-fires with ethnic armies, and restored Aung San Suu Kyi's civil liberties, allowing her to win a parliamentary seat in 2012. Her National League for Democracy then swept open elections in 2015, and she became state counsellor and de facto leader of a power-sharing government, though the military retained a constitutional grip on key ministries and a quarter of the legislature. For a few years Myanmar appeared to be emerging from isolation.

That promise was overshadowed by catastrophe in Rakhine State. In August 2017, after attacks by a Rohingya militant group, Myanmar's military launched 'clearance operations' that drove more than 700,000 Rohingya Muslims into Bangladesh. A United Nations Independent International Fact-Finding Mission concluded in 2018 that the military had carried out mass killings, gang rapes and the burning of hundreds of villages with what it called genocidal intent, and called for the prosecution of the army's commander-in-chief. The treatment of the Rohingya, a long-stateless minority, became one of the gravest human-rights crises of the era.

The opening ended abruptly. After the NLD won another landslide in November 2020, the military, alleging fraud, seized power on 1 February 2021, detaining Aung San Suu Kyi and other leaders and vesting authority in a State Administration Council under Senior General Min Aung Hlaing. Mass protests met brutal repression; Aung San Suu Kyi was later sentenced to lengthy imprisonment in trials widely condemned as politically driven. The coup ignited a nationwide armed resistance and civil war that, by 2026, has displaced millions, devastated the economy, and left Myanmar fractured between the military and a patchwork of opposition and ethnic forces.

events.title: Reforms under Thein Sein
events.summary: A quasi-civilian government begins political and economic liberalization, releasing prisoners, easing censorship, and restoring Aung San Suu Kyi's freedoms.

events.title: NLD wins free elections
events.summary: Aung San Suu Kyi's National League for Democracy wins Myanmar's first fairly contested elections; she becomes state counsellor in a government sharing power with the army.

events.title: Rohingya crisis in Rakhine State
events.summary: Military 'clearance operations' drive more than 700,000 Rohingya into Bangladesh; a UN fact-finding mission later finds evidence of genocidal violence.

events.title: Military coup
events.summary: The army seizes power, detains Aung San Suu Kyi and the elected leadership, and installs a junta under Min Aung Hlaing, triggering mass protests and civil war.

figures.name: Aung San Suu Kyi
figures.blurb: After years of house arrest she led the NLD to power and governed from 2016, but was detained and imprisoned in the 2021 coup; her tenure was clouded internationally by the Rohingya crisis.

figures.name: Min Aung Hlaing
figures.blurb: Commander-in-chief of Myanmar's armed forces who led the 2021 coup and chairs the ruling State Administration Council; named by UN investigators over the 2017 Rohingya atrocities.

pullquote.text: The treatment of the Rohingya bore the hallmarks of the gravest crimes under international law, the UN fact-finding mission concluded.
pullquote.attribution: Terra Historica, on the 2018 UN report

figures.name: Anawrahta
figures.blurb: The first king to unify Myanmar, he made Theravada Buddhism the state religion and laid the cultural foundation embodied in the temple-strewn plain of Bagan.

figures.name: Aung San
figures.blurb: The nationalist leader who steered Burma to the threshold of independence and negotiated the 1947 settlement with Britain before his assassination; revered as the country's founding father.

figures.name: Aung San Suu Kyi
figures.blurb: Aung San's daughter and the long-imprisoned face of Myanmar's democracy movement, who led an elected government from 2016 until the military removed and jailed her in 2021.

sources.label: History of Myanmar: The British in Burma, 1885-1948
A Guide to the United States' History of Recognition, Diplomatic, and Consular Relations: Burma
sources.publisher: U.S. Department of State, Office of the Historian

sources.label: UN Fact-Finding Mission on Myanmar: account of violations in Rakhine, Kachin and Shan States
sources.publisher: United Nations Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights (OHCHR)
